 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0ok","title":"EPIC: Pre-Flight Validation","description":"# Epic: Pre-Flight Validation

## Overview
Create a lightweight pre-flight check script that validates VPS readiness BEFORE running the full installer.

## Problem Statement
Users discover VPS configuration problems 15 minutes into installation, wasting significant time. Common predictable issues: wrong Ubuntu version, insufficient disk, low RAM, DNS failures.

## Business Impact
- Fail fast, fail informatively: Know in 10 seconds instead of 15 minutes
- Provider-agnostic: Works regardless of OVH/Contabo/Hetzner differences
- Increases confidence: Users know their VPS is correctly provisioned

## Technical Approach
1. Create scripts/preflight.sh (~150 lines)
2. Checks: OS version, arch, disk, RAM, swap, internet, DNS, user perms
3. Output: Human-readable with colors, --json for programmatic use
4. Wizard integration: New step between SSH Connect and Run Installer

## Success Criteria
- Catches >80% of installation failures before they happen
- Runs in <15 seconds
- Clear actionable output for each check
- Wizard updated with pre-flight step

## Files to Create/Modify
- scripts/preflight.sh (new)
- apps/web/app/wizard/preflight-check/page.tsx (new)
- apps/web/lib/wizardSteps.ts

## Reference

- generated scripts stay source-safe (no top-level side effects)","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:50:07Z"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0sb","title":"EPIC: Agent Session Sharing and Replay","description":"# Epic: Agent Session Sharing and Replay

## Overview
Enable users to export successful agent sessions and share/replay them, creating a library of reusable workflows.

## Problem Statement
When an agent workflow works well, there is no way to share it with teammates, replay it on similar problems, or learn from successful patterns. Session data exists in CASS but is not exportable.

## Business Impact
- Agent sessions are valuable artifacts (successful workflows should be reusable)
- Learning from examples > documentation (seeing what worked teaches patterns)
- Community effect (shared sessions make ACFS more valuable)
- Differentiation (other tools don't enable workflow sharing)

## Technical Approach
Phase 1 (Local):
1. Create acfs session export/import commands
2. Define export JSON schema (sanitized transcript, outcomes, key prompts)
3. Build sanitization rules (redact API keys, tokens, passwords)
4. Implement --dry-run preview for imports
5. Integrate with CASS for session retrieval

Phase 2 (Community - Future):
- Hosted backend for sharing
- Search/browse interface
- Upvote/rating system

## Success Criteria
- Sessions exportable to portable JSON format
- All secrets/credentials automatically redacted
- Import shows clear preview of what will be created/modified
- Round-trip: export then import reproduces outcomes

## Dependencies
- Requires CASS (Coding Agent Session Search) installed

## Files to Create
- scripts/lib/session.sh (new)
- acfs/session/ directory with templates

## Reference

-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0vb","title":"[ubu.5] Add preflight checks for upgrade safety","description":"# Add Preflight Checks for Upgrade Safety

## Purpose
Validate system is safe to upgrade before attempting. Prevents catastrophic failures mid-upgrade.

## Required Checks

### 1. Disk Space (ubuntu_check_disk_space)
```bash
# Require at least 5GB free on root partition
local available_gb=$(df -BG / | awk "NR==2 {print \$4}" | tr -d "G")
if [[ "$available_gb" -lt 5 ]]; then
  log_error "Insufficient disk space: ${available_gb}GB available, 5GB required"
  return 1
fi
```

### 2. Network Connectivity (ubuntu_check_network)
```bash
# Test connectivity to Ubuntu archive
if \! curl -fsS --connect-timeout 10 http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/ >/dev/null; then
  log_error "Cannot reach Ubuntu archive servers"
  return 1
fi

# Test HTTPS (needed for some repos)
if \! curl -fsS --connect-timeout 10 https://changelogs.ubuntu.com/ >/dev/null; then
  log_warn "HTTPS connectivity issues (non-fatal)"
fi
```

### 3. Not Running in Docker (ubuntu_check_not_docker)
```bash
# do-release-upgrade does not work in containers
if [[ -f /.dockerenv ]] || grep -q docker /proc/1/cgroup 2>/dev/null; then
  log_error "Ubuntu upgrade not supported in Docker containers"
  return 1
fi
```

### 4. Not Running in WSL (ubuntu_check_not_wsl)
```bash
# WSL has its own upgrade mechanism
if grep -qi microsoft /proc/version 2>/dev/null; then
  log_error "Ubuntu upgrade not supported in WSL"
  return 1
fi
```

### 5. APT State Clean (ubuntu_check_apt_state)
```bash
# Check for broken packages
if \! apt-get check 2>/dev/null; then
  log_error "APT has broken packages. Run: apt-get -f install"
  return 1
fi

# Check for held packages (may block upgrade)
local held=$(apt-mark showhold)
if [[ -n "$held" ]]; then
  log_warn "Held packages may cause upgrade issues: $held"
fi

# Check dpkg for interrupted operations
if [[ -f /var/lib/dpkg/lock-frontend ]]; then
  log_error "Another package manager is running"
  return 1
fi
```

### 6. Running as Root (ubuntu_check_root)
```bash
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
  log_error "Ubuntu upgrade requires root privileges"
  return 1
fi
```

### 7. Uptime Check (ubuntu_check_recent_boot)
```bash
# Warn if system was just rebooted (may indicate instability)
local uptime_seconds=$(cat /proc/uptime | cut -d. -f1)
if [[ "$uptime_seconds" -lt 60 ]]; then
  log_warn "System just booted. Waiting for services to stabilize..."
  sleep 30
fi
```

## Aggregate Function

```bash
ubuntu_preflight_checks() {
  local failed=0
  
  ubuntu_check_root || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_not_docker || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_not_wsl || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_disk_space || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_network || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_apt_state || failed=1
  ubuntu_check_recent_boot  # Warning only, no failure
  
  return $failed
}
```

## Design Decisions

### Why 5GB disk space?
Conservative estimate. do-release-upgrade downloads ~1-2GB of packages,
needs temp space for extraction, and the new packages may be larger.
5GB provides comfortable margin.

### Why check Docker/WSL?
These environments have their own upgrade mechanisms. Running 
do-release-upgrade would fail or cause undefined behavior.

### Why allow held packages with warning?
Some users intentionally hold packages. Blocking on this would be
too aggressive. We warn but proceed - upgrade may fail later with
clear error if held package is actually problematic.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:21:45.558198-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:21:45.558198-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0vb","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:23:35.437967-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-anq","title":"Modify verify_and_run() to use new checksum recovery flow","description":"# Task: Modify verify_and_run() to use new checksum recovery flow

## Context
Part of EPIC: Checkpoint-Based Checksum Recovery (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-tx7)

## Problem
The existing verify_and_run() in security.sh just aborts on mismatch. We have new handler logic but need to wire it in.

## Current Code (security.sh ~line 143)
```bash
verify_checksum() {
    # ... fetch and check ...
    if [[ "$actual" != "$expected" ]]; then
        log_error "Checksum mismatch"
        return 1  # Just fails
    fi
}
```

## New Code
```bash
verify_checksum_with_recovery() {
    # ... fetch and check ...
    if [[ "$actual" != "$expected" ]]; then
        handle_checksum_mismatch "$name" "$expected" "$actual" "$url"
        local result=$?
        case $result in
            0) return 0 ;;  # Skip, continue
            1) return 1 ;;  # Abort
            2) ;;           # Proceed anyway (fall through)
        esac
    fi
    # Execute
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- verify_and_run calls handle_checksum_mismatch on mismatch
- Return codes handled correctly
- Existing callers (fetch_and_run, etc.) updated
- Tests verify all three paths work

## Dependencies
Requires: handle_checksum_mismatch() to exist

## Files to Modify
- scripts/lib/security.sh","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:50:39.231304-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:01:21.489992-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:01:21.489992-05:00","close_reason":"Verified implementation: handle_checksum_mismatch() (lines 623-714) and fetch_and_run_with_recovery() (lines 305-376) already implemented in security.sh. Functions handle P/S/A prompts, CRITICAL vs optional classification, batch mode via ACFS_BATCH_CHECKSUMS, non-interactive mode.  {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aqs","title":"Create run_deep_checks() with formatted output","description":"# Task: Create run_deep_checks() with formatted output

## Context
Part of EPIC: Enhanced Doctor with Functional Tests (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-t9i)

## What to Do
Create the main deep checks runner with proper formatting:

### run_deep_checks() Function
```bash
run_deep_checks() {
    section "Functional Tests (--deep)"

    check_with_status "Claude Code auth" check_claude_auth \
        "Run: claude auth login"

    check_with_status "Codex CLI auth" check_codex_auth \
        "Set OPENAI_API_KEY in ~/.zshrc.local"

    # ... more checks
}
```

### check_with_status() Helper
Runs check function and formats result:
- Pass: ✔ green
- Fail: ✖ red with fix hint
- Skip: ○ gray "Not installed"
- Info: ℹ blue "Not configured (optional)"

### JSON Output
When --json + --deep, include deep checks in JSON.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All deep checks run in sequence
- Each check has status icon and fix hint
- Summary: "6/9 functional checks passed"
- JSON includes deep check results

## Files to Modify

-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-auf","title":"[ubu.3] Create systemd resume service","description":"# Create Systemd Resume Service

## Purpose
Create a systemd service that runs after reboot to resume the upgrade process.

## Files to Create

### Service Template: scripts/templates/acfs-upgrade-resume.service
Standard systemd oneshot service that:
- Runs after network-online.target
- Only runs if /var/lib/acfs/upgrade_state.json exists
- Executes /var/lib/acfs/upgrade_resume.sh
- Logs to journal

### Resume Script: scripts/lib/upgrade_resume.sh
Script that gets copied to /var/lib/acfs/ and runs after reboot:
1. Sources required libraries from /var/lib/acfs/lib/
2. Checks if more upgrades needed via state
3. If complete: cleanup and launch continue_install.sh
4. If not complete: run next upgrade and reboot

## Service Lifecycle

### Setup Phase (ubuntu_setup_resume)
1. Create /var/lib/acfs/ directory structure
2. Copy library files (logging.sh, ubuntu_upgrade.sh, state.sh)
3. Copy upgrade_resume.sh script
4. Generate continue_install.sh with original ACFS args
5. Install systemd service file
6. Enable service with systemctl

### Cleanup Phase (ubuntu_cleanup_resume)  
1. Disable systemd service
2. Remove service file from /etc/systemd/system/
3. Run systemctl daemon-reload
4. Remove temporary files from /var/lib/acfs/
5. Keep upgrade log for debugging

## Why Systemd Over Cron @reboot?

1. **Network ordering**: After=network-online.target ensures apt works
2. **Reliability**: Systemd is the proper init system on Ubuntu
3. **Observability**: journalctl -u acfs-upgrade-resume for debugging
4. **Control**: Easy enable/disable without editing crontab

## Security Notes

- Service runs as root (required for apt and reboot)
- All files in /var/lib/acfs/ are root-owned
- Service self-removes after completion
- No long-running privileged processes","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:20:59.646625-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:35:25.855831-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-auf","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9xt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:23:35.66045-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-auf","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-2yd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:23:36.000091-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
+{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-auf","title":"[ubu.3] Create systemd resume service","description":"# Create Systemd Resume Service

## Purpose
Create a systemd service that runs after reboot to resume the upgrade process.

## Files to Create

### Service Template: scripts/templates/acfs-upgrade-resume.service
Standard systemd oneshot service that:
- Runs after network-online.target
- Only runs if /var/lib/acfs/upgrade_state.json exists
- Executes /var/lib/acfs/upgrade_resume.sh
- Logs to journal

### Resume Script: scripts/lib/upgrade_resume.sh
Script that gets copied to /var/lib/acfs/ and runs after reboot:
1. Sources required libraries from /var/lib/acfs/lib/
2. Checks if more upgrades needed via state
3. If complete: cleanup and launch continue_install.sh
4. If not complete: run next upgrade and reboot

## Service Lifecycle

### Setup Phase (ubuntu_setup_resume)
1. Create /var/lib/acfs/ directory structure
2. Copy library files (logging.sh, ubuntu_upgrade.sh, state.sh)
3. Copy upgrade_resume.sh script
4. Generate continue_install.sh with original ACFS args
5. Install systemd service file
6. Enable service with systemctl

### Cleanup Phase (ubuntu_cleanup_resume)  
1. Disable systemd service
2. Remove service file from /etc/systemd/system/
3. Run systemctl daemon-reload
4. Remove temporary files from /var/lib/acfs/
5. Keep upgrade log for debugging

## Why Systemd Over Cron @reboot?

1. **Network ordering**: After=network-online.target ensures apt works
2. **Reliability**: Systemd is the proper init system on Ubuntu
3. **Observability**: journalctl -u acfs-upgrade-resume for debugging
4. **Control**: Easy enable/disable without editing crontab

## Security Notes

- Service runs as root (required for apt and reboot)
- All files in /var/lib/acfs/ are root-owned
- Service self-removes after completion
- No long-running privileged processes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:20:59.646625-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:37:30.059458-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:37:30.059458-05:00","close_reason":"Created systemd service template, upgrade_resume.sh script, MOTD update functions, and infrastructure setup/teardown functions.  {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bq3","title":"Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature","description":"# Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature

## Overview
Automatically detect Ubuntu version and upgrade to the latest stable release (25.10) before running the main ACFS installation. The entire process should be **fully automated and non-interactive**.

## Problem Statement
Many cloud providers (OVH, Contabo, Hetzner) default to older Ubuntu versions (22.04, 24.04). ACFS works best on the latest Ubuntu, and some packages may not be available on older versions. Currently, ACFS just warns about old versions but doesn't help upgrade.

## Solution
Add a pre-installation phase that:
1. Detects current Ubuntu version
2. Calculates upgrade path to target version
3. Automatically performs sequential do-release-upgrade operations
4. Handles reboots with automatic resume via systemd service
5. Continues ACFS installation after reaching target version

## Design Philosophy
- **Fully automated**: No user interaction required once started
- **Resilient**: Handle reboots, network issues, and failures gracefully
- **Observable**: Comprehensive logging for debugging
- **Opt-out available**: Users can skip with --skip-ubuntu-upgrade

## Default Behavior
With the standard one-liner:
```bash
curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe
```
The installer will:
1. Detect Ubuntu version
2. If below 25.10, automatically upgrade (no prompts)
3. Reboot as needed
4. Resume and continue upgrading until at target
5. Proceed with ACFS installation

## Opt-out
```bash
curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe --skip-ubuntu-upgrade
```

## Technical Constraints
1. Ubuntu only allows upgrading to NEXT version (no skipping)
   - 22.04 → 24.04 (LTS-to-LTS is special case)
   - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (sequential)
2. Each upgrade requires a reboot
3. do-release-upgrade takes 30-60 minutes per version
4. SSH sessions will disconnect during reboot (users must reconnect)
5. Need systemd service for automatic resume after reboot

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Detects Ubuntu version accurately
- [ ] Calculates correct upgrade path
- [ ] Performs upgrade non-interactively
- [ ] Creates systemd service for post-reboot resume
- [ ] Chains multiple upgrades without manual intervention
- [ ] Resumes ACFS installation after final upgrade
- [ ] Cleans up systemd service after completion
- [ ] Handles errors with clear logging
- [ ] Works across SSH disconnection/reconnection","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:18:52.892435-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:19:28.542304-05:00"}
